text: The robotic arms are used to reduce the contents of the hiatal hernia, which includes thirty percent of the proximal stomach. The surgeon enters the lesser sac at the level of the cardia, facilitating a tension-free Nissen fundoplication and improved visualization of the left crura.

The surgeon carefully preserves the peritoneum overlying the crura to optimize the hernia defect repair during cruroplasty. The focus shifts to the right side, where the gastrohepatic ligament is opened to enhance visualization of the right crura.

The surgeon carefully dissects to mobilize and free the hernia sac from its attachments to the posterior mediastinal structures, facilitating the reduction of hernia contents. The junction of the right and left crura is targeted, with the inferior vena cava visible to the left of the right crus.

The surgeon identifies the left crux and maneuvers behind the esophagus to visualize the instrument at the angle of His. The esophagus is encircled to facilitate manipulation of the gastroesophageal junction, continuing dissection within the mediastinum. The hernia sac is prominent, and complete mobilization of mediastinal structures is crucial for reducing the hernia sac and its contents. The hernia sac is removed to prevent its incorporation during the fundoplication.

The surgeon removes the hernia sac, enhancing the visualization of the gastroesophageal junction. A posterior cruroplasty is performed using a zero-ethyl bond permanent suture in a figure-of-eight pattern. The robotic platform facilitates precise manipulation of instruments, suture, and needle placement with seven degrees of freedom.

The surgeon performs an anterior cruroplasty, with a fully French bougie positioned in the esophagus and stomach for calibration. A biological mesh is introduced to support both anterior and posterior cruroplasty, visually encircling the esophagus. The mesh is secured with interrupted sutures to avoid encircling the esophagus, minimizing the risk of stretching. This technique is chosen over using tacks to prevent injury to structures beneath the diaphragm and crura.

The surgeon initiates the fundoplication by placing multiple gastro-gastric stitches over a distance of approximately two centimeters, creating the Nissen fundoplication. The earlier mobilization and treatment of the cardia allow for a tension-free completion of the procedure, with the final view showing the completed fundoplication.
